annotations.label: Curl (informal)

anchor.snippet: the curl, also known as rotor, is a vector operator that describes the infinitesimal circulation of a vector field

annotations.note: Mathlib has no curl operator on vector fields; only the more general exterior derivative `extDeriv` is defined.

annotations.label: Irrotational vector field

anchor.snippet: A vector field whose curl is zero is called irrotational

annotations.note: Without a curl definition in Mathlib, there is no corresponding notion of an irrotational vector field.

annotations.label: Stokes' theorem (curl form)

anchor.snippet: The corresponding form of the fundamental theorem of calculus is Stokes' theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib has the divergence theorem for Henstock–Kurzweil/Bochner integrals and a Poincaré lemma for 1-forms, but no Stokes theorem stated in curl form.

annotations.label: Curl operator

anchor.snippet: The curl of a vector field F, denoted by curl F

annotations.note: No declaration named `curl` (or analog) on vector fields exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curl via line integral (component definition)

anchor.snippet: the component of the curl of F along the direction

annotations.note: The line-integral characterisation of curl components is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curl at a point via boundary line integral

anchor.snippet: the curl is defined at a point p as

annotations.note: No limiting boundary-line-integral definition of curl appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kelvin–Stokes theorem

anchor.snippet: To this definition fits naturally the Kelvin–Stokes theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain a Kelvin–Stokes theorem; only the divergence theorem and a Poincaré lemma for 1-forms exist.

annotations.label: Curl via surface integral over a shell

anchor.snippet: as the limiting value of a vector-valued surface integral around a shell enclosing p divided by the volume enclosed

annotations.note: No surface-integral-over-a-shell definition of curl is present.

annotations.label: Curl as twice the rotation vector

anchor.snippet: A third way to define the curl emphasizes its interpretation as twice the rotation vector of an infinitesimal spherical volume of field

annotations.note: This interpretation as twice the infinitesimal rotation vector is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curl in curvilinear orthogonal coordinates

anchor.snippet: there is another "easy to memorize" definition of the curl in curvilinear orthogonal coordinates

annotations.note: Curvilinear orthogonal coordinates and the corresponding curl formula are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curl in Cartesian coordinates

anchor.snippet: Expanded in 3-dimensional Cartesian coordinates

annotations.note: The explicit Cartesian formula for curl is not in Mathlib because curl itself is not defined.

annotations.label: Invariance under proper rotations

anchor.snippet: the result is invariant under proper rotations of the coordinate axes but the result inverts under reflection

annotations.note: No statement about rotational invariance / parity of curl exists, as curl is not defined.

annotations.label: Curl in general coordinate system

anchor.snippet: In a general coordinate system, the curl is given by

annotations.note: No general-coordinate (Levi-Civita / metric tensor) formula for curl is formalized.

annotations.label: Curl via exterior derivative

anchor.snippet: Equivalently, using the exterior derivative, the curl can be expressed as

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the exterior derivative `extDeriv` for differential forms on normed spaces, but it is not specialized to give curl on 1-forms in ℝ³.

annotations.label: Ball in a fluid flow

anchor.snippet: Suppose the vector field describes the velocity field of a fluid flow

annotations.note: This worked example involving curl of a fluid velocity field is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rotating uniform vector field

anchor.snippet: Upon visual inspection, the field can be described as "rotating"

annotations.note: This example is not formalized; curl is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-uniform rotational field

anchor.snippet: the curl is not as obvious from the graph

annotations.note: This example computation of curl on a non-uniform field is absent.

annotations.label: Curl of rotating disk velocity field

anchor.snippet: In a vector field describing the linear velocities of each part of a rotating disk in uniform circular motion

annotations.note: No formalization of the rotating-disk velocity field example.

annotations.label: Curl of force field and torque

anchor.snippet: For any solid object subject to an external physical force

annotations.note: The torque/curl interpretation in classical mechanics is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maxwell's equations using curl

anchor.snippet: Of the four Maxwell's equations, two—Faraday's law and Ampère's law—can be compactly expressed using curl

annotations.note: Maxwell's equations are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curl of a cross product

anchor.snippet: the curl of a cross product of vector fields v and F can be shown to be

annotations.note: The vector-calculus identity for curl of a cross product is absent.

annotations.label: Cross product with curl (Feynman notation)

anchor.snippet: we arrive at the cross product of a vector field with curl of a vector field

annotations.note: Feynman-notation identity v × (∇ × F) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Curl of curl and vector Laplacian

anchor.snippet: Another example is the curl of a curl of a vector field

annotations.note: The identity ∇×(∇×F) = ∇(∇·F) − ΔF is not in Mathlib (neither is the vector Laplacian).

annotations.label: Curl of a gradient is zero

anchor.snippet: The curl of the gradient of any scalar field φ is always the zero vector field

annotations.note: The d∘d = 0 theorem `extDeriv_extDeriv` generalizes curl∘grad = 0, but the specific vector-calculus statement is not formalized.

annotations.label: Divergence of curl is zero

anchor.snippet: The divergence of the curl of any vector field is equal to zero

annotations.note: Also a special case of `extDeriv_extDeriv`, but div∘curl = 0 is not stated explicitly because div and curl are not defined.

annotations.label: Curl of scalar times vector field

anchor.snippet: If φ is a scalar valued function and F is a vector field

annotations.note: The Leibniz-style identity ∇×(φF) = ∇φ × F + φ(∇×F) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Differential k-forms in 3 dimensions

anchor.snippet: In 3 dimensions, a differential 0-form is a real-valued function

annotations.note: Mathlib represents differential `n`-forms as `E → E [⋀^Fin n]→L[𝕜] F`, generalizing the 3D 0/1/2/3-form classification.

annotations.label: Exterior derivative

anchor.snippet: The exterior derivative of a k-form in R 3 is defined as the ( k + 1) -form from above

annotations.note: `extDeriv` defines the exterior derivative of a differential form on any normed space, generalizing the ℝ³ case.

annotations.label: d squared equals zero

anchor.snippet: the twofold application of the exterior derivative yields

annotations.note: `extDeriv_extDeriv` proves d²ω = 0 for sufficiently smooth forms on normed spaces.

annotations.label: Hodge duality on R^3

anchor.snippet: this is known as Hodge duality

annotations.note: No Hodge star operator on ℝ³ (or in general) is defined in Mathlib's analysis library.

annotations.label: grad, curl, div from exterior derivative

anchor.snippet: Thus, identifying 0-forms and 3-forms with scalar fields, and 1-forms and 2-forms with vector fields

annotations.note: The exterior derivative is formalized, but its identification with grad/curl/div via Hodge duality is not, because curl, div, and Hodge star are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curl as 2-vector field (geometric)

anchor.snippet: 2-vectors correspond to the exterior power

annotations.note: The geometric interpretation of curl as a 2-vector field is not formalized.

annotations.label: Curl of 2D-dependent 3D vector field

anchor.snippet: The curl of a 3-dimensional vector field which only depends on 2 coordinates

annotations.note: This special-case reduction of curl is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Existence of vector potential

anchor.snippet: In the case where the divergence of a vector field V is zero, a vector field W exists such that

annotations.note: Mathlib has a Poincaré lemma for 1-forms on convex sets but not the existence of a vector potential for divergence-free 3D fields.

annotations.label: Non-uniqueness of inverse curl

anchor.snippet: If W is a vector field with curl( W ) = V, then adding any gradient vector field

annotations.note: The gauge ambiguity W → W + ∇m for inverse curl is not formalized.
